edges.description: Constitutive KIT signaling drives ligand-independent survival and proliferation of clonal mast cells, which accumulate in the dermis.
edges.source: Constitutive KIT Activation in Skin Mast Cells

edges.description: Constitutive KIT signaling lowers the activation threshold of mast cells, promoting degranulation and mediator release on triggering.
edges.source: Constitutive KIT Activation in Skin Mast Cells

edges.description: Increased dermal mast-cell density and associated hyperpigmentation produce the maculopapular lesions.
Histamine and other mediators released on rubbing produce pruritus.
Mediator release on stroking a lesion produces localized wheal-and-flare urtication.
Vasoactive mediator release produces episodic flushing.
Intense mediator release, especially in infants, can produce blistering of lesional skin.
Massive systemic mediator release can precipitate anaphylaxis.

edges.target: Constitutive KIT Activation in Skin Mast Cells

nodes.description: Systemic mast-cell mediator release can precipitate anaphylaxis; the risk is lower in MPCM than in diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis but warrants epinephrine auto-injector availability.

evidence.snippet: the availability of epinephrine auto-injectors for the patients to use in case of severe anaphylactic reactions are recommended

nodes.description: Constitutive KIT signaling produces a clonal accumulation of mast cells that is, by definition of cutaneous mastocytosis, limited to the skin (the dermis) without extracutaneous organ infiltration. The increased dermal mast-cell density underlies the characteristic hyperpigmented maculopapular lesions, and the absence of organ involvement distinguishes MPCM from systemic mastocytosis.

evidence.snippet: Cutaneous mastocytosis (CM), the most common form in children, is defined when MC infiltration is limited to the skin.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation in Children.
evidence.snippet: In most children, mastocytosis is limited to skin.

nodes.description: MPCM is a clonal mast-cell disease driven by somatic gain-of-function mutations of KIT, the receptor tyrosine kinase for stem cell factor (CD117). The mutations render KIT constitutively active and ligand independent, driving downstream MAPK and PI3K/AKT pro-survival and proliferative signaling. Whereas adult and systemic disease is dominated by the activation-loop mutation D816V (exon 17), children with cutaneous disease carry a broader spectrum of activating KIT mutations, frequently outside codon 816 (e.g. extracellular exon 8/9 and juxtamembrane exon 11), which has direct therapeutic consequences because D816V is imatinib resistant while many non-codon-816 variants are imatinib sensitive.
nodes.id: Constitutive KIT Activation in Skin Mast Cells

evidence.reference_title: Pediatric mastocytosis is a clonal disease associated with D816V and other activating c-KIT mutations.
evidence.snippet: All mutations identified in this study were somatic and caused a constitutive activation of c-KIT.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation in Children.
evidence.snippet: Children with cutaneous mastocytosis typically exhibit mutations in various regions of the KIT gene, whereas those with systemic disease predominantly carry KIT D816V.
Genetic aberrations, mainly the KIT D816V mutation, play a crucial role in the pathogenesis of mastocytosis, enhancing MC survival and subsequent accumulation in organs and tissues.

nodes.description: Blistering (bullae) of lesional skin can occur, especially in infants and in severe polymorphic or diffuse disease, from intense mediator release.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation in Children.
evidence.snippet: Many pediatric patients suffer from symptoms of mast cell activation, ranging from pruritus to flushing and blistering.

nodes.description: Stroking or rubbing a lesion elicits localized whealing and erythema (the Darier sign) due to mast-cell mediator release; it is a pathognomonic and near-universal clinical sign, although a negative Darier sign does not exclude mastocytosis.

evidence.reference_title: Cutaneous mastocytosis in children: a clinical analysis of 71 cases.
evidence.snippet: Darier's sign was present in 94% of cases.

evidence.reference_title: Challenges in the Diagnosis of Cutaneous Mastocytosis.
evidence.snippet: Typical CM manifestations (MPCM), including mastocytoma or diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis (DCM), all share a positive Darier's sign, and can thus be clinically recognized.

nodes.description: Episodic flushing from vasoactive mast-cell mediator release.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation in Children.
evidence.snippet: Many pediatric patients suffer from symptoms of mast cell activation, ranging from pruritus to flushing and blistering.

nodes.description: Multiple hyperpigmented (red-brown) maculopapular skin lesions are the defining feature of MPCM (urticaria pigmentosa) and the most common cutaneous presentation of pediatric mastocytosis.

evidence.reference_title: Paediatric mastocytosis: a systematic review of 1747 cases.
evidence.snippet: presented as urticaria pigmentosa (75% of cases), mastocytoma (20%) or diffuse cutaneous mastocytosis (5%)
The most common form is the maculopapular cutaneous mastocytosis (MPCM), formerly called urticaria pigmentosa.

nodes.description: Mast cells degranulate and release preformed and newly synthesized mediators (histamine, tryptase, prostaglandins, leukotrienes, cytokines), most characteristically when lesions are stroked or rubbed. Mediator release drives the symptomatic manifestations of MPCM, including the urticating Darier sign, pruritus, flushing, blistering in severe infantile disease, and, less commonly, systemic mast-cell-activation symptoms up to anaphylaxis.

evidence.snippet: Children with mastocytosis often suffer from MC mediator-related symptoms, the most common of which is itching, often triggered by rubbing the lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Management of Mastocytosis and Mast Cell Activation in Children.
evidence.snippet: Many pediatric patients suffer from symptoms of mast cell activation, ranging from pruritus to flushing and blistering.

nodes.description: Itching is the most common mast-cell-mediator symptom, characteristically triggered by rubbing the lesions.

evidence.snippet: Children with mastocytosis often suffer from MC mediator-related symptoms, the most common of which is itching, often triggered by rubbing the lesions.

evidence.reference_title: Pediatric mastocytosis is a clonal disease associated with D816V and other activating c-KIT mutations.
evidence.snippet: A mutation of codon 816 (exon 17) was found in 42% of cases, and mutations outside exon 17 were observed in 44%.

evidence.reference_title: Pediatric mastocytosis is a clonal disease associated with D816V and other activating c-KIT mutations.
evidence.snippet: although pediatric mastocytosis can spontaneously regress, it is a clonal disease most commonly associated with activating mutations in c-KIT

evidence.reference_title: Paediatric mastocytosis: a systematic review of 1747 cases.
evidence.snippet: KIT D816V mutation was detected in 34% of 215 tested patients.
